1.description: Net buying and selling by S&P 500 index trackers, by year. Net buying and selling are defined as the total change in split-adjusted shares held by index trackers between the quarter before and the quarter after the index change, divided by split-adjusted shares outstanding (multiplied by 100). Each point represents an equal-weighted average among events, by year. The red line represents a LOWESS filter with bandwidth equal to 0.8.

2.description: Average index effect by year. For each event, we compute the cumulative abnormal return over the period of interest. Lines represent 10-year moving averages of the yearly averages, expressed in percentage points.

3.description: Net purchases and the index effect. The vertical axis represents the total inclusion effect return, that is, the abnormal return from the day before the announcement to the day after implementation, in percentage points. Each point represents an individual addition or deletion event. Net purchases are defined as the total change in split-adjusted shares held by index trackers between the quarter before and the quarter after the index change, divided by split-adjusted shares outstanding (multiplied by 100).

4.description: Migrations as a fraction of S&P 500 additions and deletions. Each year, we define migration additions as firms that are simultaneously added to the S&P 500 and dropped from the S&P MidCap, while migration deletions are firms that are simultaneously dropped from the S&P 500 and added to the S&P MidCap. Each data point represents the share of additions and deletions in our sample each year that are classified as migrations. Lines represent 10-year moving averages.

5.description: Percent of S&P MidCap 400 owned by index trackers. Each year, we identify S&P MidCap 400 index funds based on names and correlations. To identify funds based on names, we require that the fund name contain either variants of “S&P,” “SPDR,” or “S and P” as well as variants of “400” or “MidCap.” To identify funds based on correlations, we first restrict attention to the universe of midcap-focused equity funds (those with either CRSP objective code “EDCM” or a Lipper objective code that starts with MC) that do not include variants of “Vanguard” or “Russell” in the name. Among these funds, we classify them as S&P MidCap 400 trackers if their returns have a correlation of at least 99.5% with the index itself for three years in a row. Finally, we add up the total assets of these funds and divide them by the total market capitalization of the S&P MidCap 400 index. The line represents a 10-year moving average.

6.description: Comparing addition and deletion returns across index migrations and “direct additions” or “direct deletions.” For each event, we compute the abnormal return from the day before the announcement to the day after implementation. The dots represent the average abnormal return each year, expressed in percentage points. The lines represent 10-year moving averages.

7.description: Cumulative abnormal preaddition and predeletion market-adjusted returns. The figure shows average cumulative abnormal returns in event time, pooled for 1980 to 1989, 1990 to 1999, 2000 to 2009, and 2010 to 2020. Event time of zero denotes the announcement date. Returns are normalized to start at zero 100-trading days before the announcement and are expressed in percentage points.

8.description: Rank of additions among stocks outside the S&P 500 index by year. Each month, we rank all ordinary common shares traded on major exchanges outside the S&P 500 by market capitalization. Then, each year, we plot the average rank as well as the 25th and 75th percentile ranks of stocks that ended up being added the month before their index addition.

9.description: Value-weighted average effective bid-ask spreads and implementation shortfall. The percentage effective spread is computed using TAQ data and the method in Holden and Jacobson (2014). The red line plots the value-weighted percentage effective spread among all ordinary common shares traded on major exchanges, that is, the weights are proportional to each firm's one-month lagged market capitalization. The effective spread is computed using second-based TAQ data from 1993 until millisecond TAQ data become available in 2003. Implementation shortfall—the blue line—is the difference, or slippage, between the arrival price and the execution price for a trade. The plot shows the average implementation shortfall from 2009 through 2021, using data from ITG and Virtu Financial, for midcap stocks.
